food_subgroup: Steamed rice noodle rolls served with sweet dark soy sauce, sesame, and fried shallots

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Singapore hawker-style chee cheong fun. Closest parent would be a steamed rice noodle dish. No defensible exact URI available; null is honest.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dishes — steamed rice noodle rolls (closest parent category in FoodEx2 grain-based composite dishes)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Singapore hawker chee cheong fun with sweet dark sauce. A000E (rice-based dishes) is the closest defensible parent. Exact code not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific hawker preparation. Not applicable for clinical coding purposes.

preparation_method: drizzled with sweet dark soy sauce
topped with fried shallots and sesame seeds
allergens: gluten (wheat starch in noodle and soy sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore hawker portion of chee cheong fun is approximately 160 g, comprising steamed rice noodle rolls drizzled with sweet dark soy sauce, sesame oil, fried shallots, and sesame seeds.

nutrient_source.title: Chee Cheong Fun (hawker, with sweet sauce) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for hawker-style chee cheong fun with sweet dark sauce. Values represent the complete dish as served including sauce. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for rice noodle analogues where HPB data was not available; confidence 0.9 for those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — rice noodle analogues; Atkinson et al. 2008 / University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodles (steamed, with sweet sauce)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore hawker chee cheong fun with sweet dark sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: steamed rice noodles (GI ~61–68 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The sweet dark soy sauce adds simple sugars that elevate the effective GI toward the upper range (~65). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 44.5g carbohydrate in 160g serving) / 100 = 28.9. This is classified as high GL (>20). Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 281 mg/100 g; approximately 450 mg sodium per standard 160 g serving from sweet dark soy sauce and hoisin sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request sauce on the side and use sparingly; if optional prawn paste (har cheong) is added, sodium per serving can reach approximately 750 mg, which represents 33–50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load of 28.9 per 160 g serving driven by refined rice flour noodles (GI ~65) and 15–20 g added sugar from sweet dark soy and hoisin sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: T2DM patients should be counselled that the sweet dark sauce — not the rice noodle itself — is the primary glycemic driver; requesting sauce on the side and using minimally, or substituting with savoury chilli sambal, can substantially reduce the glycemic load; portion reduction or replacement with a higher-protein breakfast is recommended for those with poor glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g) and zero trans fat; primary cardiovascular concern is sodium from the sweet dark sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it relatively favourable for cardiovascular disease from a lipid perspective; however, the sodium content of approximately 450 mg per serving warrants moderation for patients with concurrent hypertension or heart failure, and the high sugar load from the sauce should be considered in the context of metabolic cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat; sesame oil provides omega-6 fatty acids and sesame lignans with modest in-vitro anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dish is low in pro-inflammatory saturated fat; the primary RA-relevant concern is sodium from the sauce, which may interact with NSAID therapy; patients on NSAIDs should moderate sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chee Cheong Fun (猪肠粉, Zhū Cháng Fěn) is a beloved Singapore hawker breakfast and snack consisting of silky smooth, translucent steamed rice noodle rolls made from rice flour and wheat starch. In the Singapore hawker context, the rolls are served plain (without filling), cut into bite-sized pieces, and generously drizzled with a sweet dark sauce combining hoisin sauce and sweet soy sauce, finished with sesame oil, toasted sesame seeds, and crispy fried shallots. Optional accompaniments include sesame paste and prawn paste (har cheong). The dish is prized for its silky, slightly elastic texture and the contrast between the neutral rice noodle and the intensely sweet-savoury sauce. Despite appearing light, one standard 160 g hawker portion carries a glycemic load of approximately 28.9 — among the highest of common hawker breakfast items — driven primarily by the sweet sauce contributing 15–20 g of sugar per serving. The rice noodle itself has a GI of approximately 55–61 without sauce, but the sweet dark sauce raises the effective GI to approximately 65. Clinically, the key teaching point for T2DM counselling is that the sauce, not the noodle, is the primary glycemic and sugar concern. The dish is low in saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g), cholesterol-free, and suitable for vegetarian, vegan, and halal diets. Sodium per serving is approximately 450 mg, rising to approximately 750 mg if prawn paste is added. Available at virtually all Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, most commonly as a breakfast or afternoon snack item.
common_user_queries: rice noodle roll sweet sauce GI

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and secondary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed data for steamed rice noodles, with the sweet sauce adjustment documented. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogues at confidence 0.9. Overall record confidence is high given the strong primary source alignment for all core and secondary nutrients.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Singapore hawker chee cheong fun with sweet dark sauce is not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific hawker preparation is not reported in the HPB database. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source). 4. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — plain rice noodle rolls with plant-based sauce contain no animal-derived cholesterol). 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — no significant carotenoid or retinol sources in plain rice noodle with sweet soy sauce). 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal products). 7. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no UV-exposed mushrooms or animal products present). 8. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on steamed rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct measurement exists for this specific Singapore hawker preparation. 9. Portions: the default serving is 160 g (HPB-verified); a 200 g serving size also appears in enriched source data but is not used as default. 10. Copper (0.05 mg/100g), manganese (0.05 mg/100g), and vitamin K (0.5 mcg/100g) values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy rice noodle analogues at confidence 0.9 where HPB data was unavailable. 11. FoodEx2 code A000E is used as the closest defensible parent category; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Singapore hawker chee cheong fun.
